← World Gallery
Regions Artists Timeline All works

East Asia

China, Japan, Korea — from terracotta armies to woodblock waves.

East Asian art has a long memory. Forms travel — from Chinese brushwork to Japanese ukiyo-e, from Tang Buddhism to Korean celadon — and each reinvention carries the earlier one inside it.

Click any image to enlarge. Use ← → or close with Esc.

Before 1000 BCE

Amulet in the Form of a Seated Figure with Bovine Head
Northeast China, Neolithic period, probably Hongshan culture (4700–2920 BCE) · c. 4700–2920 BCE · jade (nephrite)
Ceremonial Disk-Axe (Yue)
Northeast China, Neolithic period, Hongshan culture (4700–2920 BCE) · c. 4700–2920 BCE · serpentine stone
Jar with Spiral Designs
Northwest China, Neolithic period, Majiayao culture, Majiayao phase (3300–2650 BCE) · 3300–2650 BCE · earthenware with slip-painted decoration
Ceremonial Disc (Bi)
East China, Neolithic period, Liangzhu culture (3300–2200 BCE) · 3300–2200 BCE · jade (nephrite)
Urn with Triangular Patterns
Northwest China, Neolithic period, Majiayao culture · c. 3300–c. 2000 BCE · Earthenware with slip-painted decoration
Cylinder
China · ca. 3200 BC-2200 BC
Jōmon Flame Pottery
Jōmon · c. 3000 BCE · Japan
Clay pots with rims twisted into exuberant "flame" shapes. Made by prehistoric hunter-gatherers — and, at 15,000 years old, among the oldest pottery traditions on earth.
Jade Cong
Liangzhu · c. 3300–2300 BCE · China
Square outside, round inside — the cong was a ritual jade of the Liangzhu culture, buried with elites by the hundreds. Its cosmology is still debated, its method of drilling still not fully explained.
Bird-shaped Urn
Northwest China, Neolithic period, Majiayao culture, Banshan phase (2650–2350 BCE) · 2650–2350 BCE · earthenware with slip-painted decoration
Jar with Curvilinear Designs
Northwest China, Neolithic period, Majiayao culture, Banshan phase (2650–2350 BCE) · 2650–2350 BCE · earthenware with slip-painted decoration
Cylinder (cong)
China · ca. 2500 BC
Half-disc (huang)
China · ca. 2500 BC
Disc (bi)
China · ca. 2500 BC
Dogū
Jōmon · 3rd millennium BCE · Japan
Clay humanoid figurines with huge goggling eyes and elaborate patterning. Deliberately broken in most cases — possibly in rites to transfer illness out of the body, or funerary substitutes.
Tripod (Ding)
China, Western Zhou dynasty (c. 1046–771 BCE) · 1000s BCE · bronze
Ceremonial Blade with Three Perforations (Dao)

Ring

Wine Beaker (Gu)
China, Shang dynasty (c. 1600–c. 1046 BCE), Anyang phase (c. 1250–1046 BCE) · c. 1200 BCE · bronze
Sanxingdui Bronze Masks
Sanxingdui culture · c. 1200 BCE · Sichuan
Hollow bronze and gold masks with protruding eyes and ears that fan outward. An entirely unknown civilization contemporary with Shang, found in two sacrificial pits in 1986.
Sanxingdui Gold Mask
Sanxingdui · c. 1200 BCE · Sichuan
A beaten gold mask from a sacrificial pit unknown to any historical text. The Sanxingdui culture was erased from memory; their spectacular bronzes only rediscovered in the 1980s.
Oracle Bone
Shang · c. 1200 BCE · China
Ox scapulae and turtle plastrons inscribed with questions for the royal ancestors — will the harvest come? will the tooth-ache end? Cracks were heated into the bone; the answer was in the break.
Fu Hao Tomb Bronzes
Shang · c. 1200 BCE · Anyang
Found sealed in the grave of a Shang queen-general — 1,600 bronzes, jades, and bones. Fu Hao led armies and conducted state sacrifices, and was buried with the ritual ware of a ruler.
Tripod Cauldron (Ding)
China, Shang dynasty (c. 1600–c. 1046 BCE) · 1200–1100 BCE · bronze
Tripod Cauldron of Ran (Ran ding)
by China

Li, Hollow-Legged Tripod
China, Inner Mongolia, lower stratum of the Xiajiadian culture (2200–1600 BCE) · 1200–800 BCE · dark gray earthenware
Goblet
China · 1150 BC-1050 BC
Bronze vessel (ding)
China · 1100 BC -1000 BC
Wine Container
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · -1099–-1000 · Bronze
Shang Ritual Altar Set
Shang China · late 11th c. BCE
A complete bronze altar table with fourteen vessels arranged on it — wine containers, food bowls, ceremonial cups. Whole sets are rare; this one survived together because it was buried together.
Yu Wine Container
Shang China · late 11th c. BCE
A bulbous bronze wine container with swing handle, flanged with taotie monster-masks. Each piece of Shang ritual bronze is inscribed for the person it was cast to commemorate.
Cauldron
by China

1000 BCE – 0

Hollow-Legged Tripod (Li Ding)
China, Shaanxi province, Xi'an, Western Zhou dynasty (c. 1046–771 BCE) · late 900 BCE · bronze
Chinese Ritual Bronzes
Shang–Zhou · 16th–3rd c. BCE
Tripod cauldrons, wine vessels, and axes cast for ancestor worship. Each is wrapped in a monster mask (taotie) staring from every surface.
Bell (Lai Zhong)
China, Shaanxi province, Meixian, Western Zhou dynasty (c. 1046–771 BCE) · c. 800–700 BCE · bronze
Handle of a Jiangu Drum

Finial

Cranes and Serpents
China, reportedly from Hunan province, Changsha, Warring States period (475–221 BCE), State of Chu (c. 1046–223 BCE) · 475–221 BCE · lacquered wood with polychromy
Pouring vessel
China · 450 BC-250 BC
Bells of Marquis Yi of Zeng
Warring States · c. 433 BCE · China
A rack of 65 tuned bronze bells from a duke's tomb, each capable of two distinct tones depending on where struck. A full orchestra buried in the ground — still playable after 2,400 years.
Mirror with Four T's
China, Warring States period (475–221 BCE) · 300s BCE · bronze
Double-tier Mirror
China, Warring States period (475–221 BCE) · 300s BCE · bronze
Mirror
China · BC 300-200
Terracotta Army
Qin dynasty · 210 BCE · Xi'an, China
Over 8,000 soldiers, each with a different face, buried with the first emperor of China to guard him after death. Farmers digging a well found them in 1974.
Relief with Dragon from a Funerary Stove Model
China, from a tomb in Xi'an, Shaanxi province, Han dynasty (202 BCE–220 CE) · 202 BCE–220 CE · earthenware with relief decoration
Dragon Head Ladle

China · Chinese (culture or style) · -200–-101 · Gray earthenware
Incense Burner (Boshan Lu)
China, Western Han dynasty (206 BCE–8 CE) · 200–100 BCE · bronze
Funeral Banner of Lady Dai
Western Han · c. 168 BCE · Mawangdui
A T-shaped silk banner painted with the heavens, the earth, and the underworld, hung over the corpse of Lady Dai — whose own body was preserved so intact that forensic anthropologists have autopsied her.
Jade Burial Suit
Western Han · 2nd c. BCE · China
Thousands of jade plaques sewn with gold wire into a full-body armor — jade was thought to preserve the dead. The one found around Prince Liu Sheng in 1968 took 2,498 tiles and years of stitching.
Panel from Model Cooking Stove: Fairy Feeding Lingzhi Fungus to a Dragon
China, from a tomb in Xi'an, Shaanxi province, Western Han dynasty (206 BCE–8 CE) · 100–1 BCE · earthenware with impressed relief decoration

China, from a tomb in Xi'an, Shaanxi province, Western Han dynasty (206 BCE–8 CE) · 100–1 BCE · earthenware with impressed relief decoration

Mirror with Deities, Chariot, and the White Tiger
China, Eastern Han dynasty (25–220 CE) · 100–200 CE · bronze
Flying Horse of Gansu
Eastern Han · 2nd c. CE · China
A bronze horse galloping on a single hoof — perched atop a flying swallow. Unearthed from a Han tomb in Wuwei; so emblematic it is now the national tourism symbol of China.
Jar with Horn-Shaped Handles
Korea, Silla (57 BCE–935 CE) · 200s–400s CE · earthenware with incised decoration and color
Jar with Loop Handle
Korea, Baekje kingdom (18 BCE–660 CE) · 200s–300s CE · earthenware

Funerary Urn (Hunping)
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 265–316 · Stoneware with olive-green glaze and molded and applied decoration
Figure of warrior
China · ca.300
Lantingji Xu (Preface to the Orchid Pavilion)
by Wang Xizhi
Eastern Jin · Wang Xizhi · 353 CE · China
A drunkenly perfect calligraphic masterpiece, 324 characters describing a riverside gathering of poets. Wang Xizhi tried to rewrite it next day and could not match it. Later buried with an emperor.
Admonitions Scroll
by Gu Kaizhi
Six Dynasties · Gu Kaizhi (copy) · c. 380 CE
A handscroll of court ladies illustrating a Confucian poem of proper conduct. Probably the oldest surviving Chinese narrative painting, preserved in a Tang-era copy.
Nymph of the Luo River
by Gu Kaizhi
Gu Kaizhi (attr.) · c. 400 CE · Song copy at the Palace Museum, Beijing
A handscroll illustrating Cao Zhi's poem about his unrequited love for a river nymph. Figures float on waves rendered in iron-wire line, backgrounds compressed into pictographic mountains — an early statement of the Chinese approach to narrative through suggestion.
Haniwa Horse
Japan, Kofun period (300–710 CE) · 400s–500s CE · earthenware with traces of pigment
Pedestal vessel
Korea · 400-600
Haniwa in the Form of a Shield
Japan, Kofun period (300–710 CE) · c. 500 · Earthenware with applied, cut, and incised designs and red slip
Northern Wei Buddha Maitreya
Northern Wei · 486 CE · China
A dated gilt-bronze Maitreya, made "in the tenth year of Taihe" (486). One of the earliest firmly dated Chinese Buddhist bronzes — the Gupta style already translated to a Chinese idiom.
Yungang Grottoes
Northern Wei · 460–525 CE · Datong
Fifty-three caves with 51,000 statues. The earliest cliff Buddhas in China — faces still showing Indian and Central Asian lineage.
Longmen Grottoes
Northern Wei → Tang · 493 CE onward · Luoyang
Over 100,000 Buddhas carved into limestone cliffs along the Yi River. The largest is fifty-six feet tall and smiles.

500 – 1000

Crown of Silla
Silla kingdom · 5th–6th c. · Korea
Thin gold sheet cut into tree and antler forms, hung with comma-shaped jades and gold spangles. Too fragile to wear — meant for ritual and burial.
Haniwa
Kofun period · 3rd–6th c. CE · Japan
Hollow terracotta cylinders and figures set in rings around imperial tumuli. Armored warriors, horses, dancers, shrine maidens — the only accessible record of pre-literate Japanese dress.
Mortuary Figure of the Zodiac Sign: Ox (Taurus)

by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 575–600 · Marble with traces of metal fittings at crown
Mirror with Interlacing Floral Motifs and Twelve Zodiac Animals
China, Sui dynasty (581-618) · late 500s–early 600s · bronze
Baekje Gilt-bronze Incense Burner
Baekje · c. 600 CE · Korea
A one-meter incense burner with a dragon base and a phoenix on top, its domed lid covered in mountains, immortals, musicians, and beasts. Pulled intact from the mud of a temple site in Buyeo in 1993.
Funerary urn and cover
Korea · 600-800
Hōryū-ji
Asuka period · c. 607 CE · Nara, Japan
Among the oldest wooden buildings still standing anywhere in the world. Built when Buddhism had only recently reached Japan.
Emperor Taizong Receiving the Tibetan Envoy
by Yan Liben
Yan Liben · 641 CE · Palace Museum, Beijing
The Tang emperor receives Lu Dongzan, envoy of Tibetan King Songtsen Gampo, who came to arrange the royal marriage that would seal East Asian diplomacy for centuries. Yan Liben was court painter and prime minister simultaneously.
Fang Xuanling Pedestal
China, Tang dynasty (618-907) · 647 · marble
Pensive Bodhisattva
Silla · 7th c. CE · Korea
A gilt-bronze figure of Maitreya seated in contemplation, one leg crossed, a finger to the chin. National Treasure No. 78 — one of the subtlest sculptures of East Asia.
Funerary urn
Korea · 650-800
Tomb figure
China · 675-725
Octafoil Mirror with Hunters and Prey

China, probably Shaanxi province, Xi'an, Tang dynasty (618-907) · early 700s · glazed earthenware, sancai (three-color) ware

China, Tang dynasty (618-907) · 700s · bronze

A Large Painted Striding Horse
China, Tang dynasty (618–907) · 700–800s · Pottery with white slip and mineral colors
Bead
Korea, Unified Silla period (676-935) · 700s · glass
Necklace
Korea, Unified Silla period (676-935) · 700s · jade, amethyst, gold, rock crystal
Standing Avalokitesvara
Korea, Unified Silla period (668-935) · 700s · Gilt-bronze
Kōfuku-ji
Nara period onward · 710 · Japan
One of Nara's four great monasteries. Its treasure hall holds the Asura statue — a three-faced, six-armed youth with an achingly thoughtful face.
Fushimi Inari-taisha
Heian period onward · 711 CE · Kyoto
Ten thousand vermillion torii gates tunnel up a sacred mountain. Each is donated by a business praying for success; the writing on the back is the donor's name.
Leshan Giant Buddha
Tang China · 713–803 · Sichuan
A seated Maitreya carved directly into a sandstone cliff at the confluence of three rivers. Seventy-one meters tall; his ears alone are seven meters. Built, a monk said, to calm the waters.
Equestrienne
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 725–750 · Earthenware with polychrome pigments
Ashura of Kōfuku-ji
Nara Japan · 734 CE
A dry-lacquer figure of a three-faced, six-armed Ashura, somehow childlike and sorrowful rather than fierce. Cast from the imagined attendants of the Buddha, preserved in the Kōfuku-ji treasure hall.
Woman Holding Plum Blossoms
North China, Tang dynasty (618-907) · mid-700s · earthenware covered in white slip with traces of pigment
Harpist
North China, Tang dynasty (618-907) · mid-700s · glazed earthenware
Night-Shining White
by Han Gan
Tang dynasty · Han Gan · c. 750 · China
A tethered stallion struggling at the post — snorting, hooves kicking. The emperor Xuanzong's favorite horse, painted in ink so alive it almost leaves the silk.
Spring Outing of the Tang Court
by Zhang Xuan
Tang Dynasty · attributed to Zhang Xuan · 8th c.
A later copy of a lost Zhang Xuan handscroll: the imperial court rides out on a spring morning in a tight file of horses. The women in the vanguard seem to pull the viewer along.
Tang Sancai
Tang dynasty · 7th–10th c. · China
"Three-color" lead-glazed earthenware — amber, green, cream — used for tomb figurines of camels, merchants, horses, ladies. A glittering snapshot of cosmopolitan Silk Road Chang'an.
Tang Dancer Figurine
Tang China · 7th–9th c. CE
Ceramic figures of courtly women with long sleeves mid-swing, faces carefully painted. Burial goods for wealthy tombs along the Silk Road — a snapshot of cosmopolitan Chang'an society.
Bulguksa
Unified Silla · 751 CE · Gyeongju, Korea
A Buddhist temple with twin pagodas (Dabotap and Seokgatap) that encode two sutras in stone form. Burned by the Japanese in 1593, rebuilt in the 1970s on the original foundations.
Dabotap Pagoda
Unified Silla · 751 · Bulguksa, Korea
An ornate twin to the plainer Seokgatap across the courtyard. Two pagodas encoding the Lotus Sutra's doctrine that every Buddha is present in every other.
Tōdai-ji
Nara period · 752 · Japan
For a thousand years the largest wooden building in the world. Inside, a 50-foot bronze Buddha, cast in eight pieces, seated in a lotus.
Shōsōin Repository
Nara period · 756 CE · Nara, Japan
A log-cabin-style storehouse of Tōdai-ji that has preserved nine thousand personal belongings of Emperor Shōmu — lutes, masks, glass bowls from Sasanian Persia, a rhinoceros-horn comb — under near-perfect humidity for 1,300 years.
Stone Panel for Royal Tomb
Korea, Unified Silla period (676-935) · late 700s-early 800s · granite
Seated Bodhisattva
by Japan
Japan · Japanese (culture or style) · 770–780 · Wood core
Seokguram Grotto
Unified Silla · 774 · Korea
A domed granite chamber holding a single seated Buddha, two and a half meters tall. The walls are carved with bodhisattvas and disciples — a sculptural mandala.
Fragment (Dress Fabric)
by China
China · tang · 775–825 · Silk
Kyoto Imperial Palace
Heian → Edo · 794–1868 · Kyoto
The residence of the emperor of Japan from 794 until 1869. Repeatedly burned and rebuilt; the current compound follows the Heian original, walled off in parkland in central Kyoto.
Sun Bodhisattva (Nikkō Bosatsu)
Japan, Heian period (794–1185) · c. 800 · Japanese nutmeg-yew wood with traces of color and gold
Cinerary Urn with Amida's Pure Land
Japan, Heian period (794–1185) · 800s-900s · gilt bronze
Cinerary Urn with Amida’s Pure Land (lid)
Japan, Heian period (794–1185) · 800s-900s · gilt bronze
Buddhist Pagoda
by Korea
Korea · Korean (culture or style) · 800–999 · Granite
Cinerary Urn with Amida’s Pure Land
Japan, Heian period (794–1185) · 800s-900s · gilt bronze
Standing Amitabha
Korea, Unified Silla period (676-935) · 800s · gilt bronze
Statue of Amitabha
Korea, Unified Silla period (676-935) · 800s · gilt bronze
Fragment
by Central Asia
Central Asia · tang · 801–1000 · Silk
Mogao Caves (Dunhuang)
Silk Road Buddhist · 4th–14th c. · Gansu
492 caves carved into cliff face, filled with murals and gilded clay Buddhas. One sealed chamber held 50,000 manuscripts — the largest trove of its kind.
Vase with Floral Scrolls
China, Henan province, Northern Song dynasty (960-1127) · 900s–1000s · glazed stoneware with incised and slip-inlaid decoration, Cizhou ware
Ewer with a Lion-shaped Spout
China, Shaanxi province, Tongchuan, Huangbaozhen, Five Dynasties period (907-960) or Northern Song dynasty (960-1126) · 900s–1000s · green-glazed porcelaneous stoneware with modeled, carved and incised decoration, Yaozhou ware
Pair of Woman's Boots with Tying Ribbon
by China
China · liao · 900–1000 · Outer boot: silk
Shinto Deity
Japan, Heian period (794–1185) · 900s · wood, with traces of polychromy
Shinto Deities
Japan, Heian period (794–1185) · 900s · wood, with traces of polychromy
Jar with Fluted Rim
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 900–999 · Ding ware; glazed porcelain
Ding Ware Bowl of the Xing Type with Bi-Disc Foot
China, Henan province, Gongxian kilns, Five dynasties (907–60) · 907–60 · White stoneware with ivory-white glaze
Woman's Robe
China, Liao dynasty (907-1125) · 907–1125 · Silk and gold thread: embroidery on silk gauze ground; silk lining, silk batting
Spoon
Korea · 918-1392
Huang Quan Bird Painting
by Huang Quan
Five Dynasties · Huang Quan · c. 950 CE
Meticulously observed birds, insects, and turtles. Huang Quan was a palace painter in Sichuan; his "sketches from life" became the model for Northern Song nature painting.
Cloud-Shaped Pillow
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 950–999 · Cizhou ware; stoneware
Flat Dish Made of Marbled Clay
North or Central China, Northern Song (960–1127) or Jin dynasty (1115–1234) · 960–1234 · Stoneware with marbled clay and transparent glaze
Tea Bowl with "Hare's fur" Glaze
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 960–1279 · Stoneware with dark brown "hare's fur" glaze and molded applied decoration
Buddhist Retreat by Stream and Mountains
by Juran
China, Northern Song dynasty (960-1127) · 960–85 · Hanging scroll; ink on silk
Bell Inscribed with the Heart Sutra
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 960–1127 · Jade
Cruciform Mirror with Six Blossoms
China, Northern Song dynasty (960-1127) · 960–1127 · bronze
Meiping Vase with Carved Floral Sprays
China, Song dynasty (960–1279) · 960–1279 · Porcelain with pale bluish-white glaze, qingbai ("blue-white") ware
Circular Shard
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 960–1279 · Jun ware; stoneware with light blue and purple glaze
Ovoid Ewer
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 960–1127 · Ding ware; glazed porcelain
Dish with Dragons, Peonies, and Stylized Clouds
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 960–1279 · Ding ware; porcelain with underglaze molded decoration; copper rim

by China

Cloud-Shaped Pillow with Peony Scroll
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 960–1050 · Cizhou ware; stoneware
Sherd
China · 960-1279
Night Revels of Han Xizai
by Gu Hongzhong
Gu Hongzhong · c. 970 CE · Palace Museum, Beijing
A Southern Tang minister who suspects he is being watched throws louder and louder parties; the emperor sends Gu Hongzhong to spy, who records five scenes of the ensuing orgy. The surviving Song copy is one of the finest figural handscrolls in Chinese art.
Head of Luohan
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 999–1099 · Hollow dry lacquer

1000 – 1500

Travelers Among Mountains and Streams
by Fan Kuan
Northern Song · Fan Kuan · c. 1000 CE
A seven-foot hanging scroll — a sheer cliff dominates two-thirds of the picture. Tiny travelers and mules cross the foreground, dwarfed. Widely considered the greatest Chinese landscape painting.
Tobatsu Bishamonten
Heian Japan · 10th–11th c.
A standing wooden Bishamonten — warrior of the four heavenly kings — pinning a small earth demon beneath his foot. The form, Chinese in origin, arrived in Japan via the Silk Road and stayed.
Cloud-Collar Pillow with Waves
China, Jiangxi Province, Northern Song dynasty (960–1127) · 1000s–1100s · glazed porcelain with incised and carved decoration
Shallow Dish with Peony Sprays
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 1000–1099 · Ding ware; porcelain with underglaze carved decoration; metal rim
Lobed Dish with Lotus Sprays and Curled Feather-Like Leaves
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 1000–1099 · Ding ware; porcelain with underglaze carved decoration
Bowl with Mandarin Ducks among Leaves and Cattails
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 1000–1099 · Ding ware; porcelain with underglaze carved and incised decoration; metal rim
Vase with Peony Decoration
China, Northern Song dynasty (960-1127) · 1000s-1100s · Gray stoneware with underglaze slip decoration, Cizhou ware
Ovoid Bottle with “Partridge-Feather” Mottles
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 1000–1127 · Cizhou ware; stoneware with overglaze slip decoration
Head of a Luohan
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 1001–1100 · Hollow dry lacquer
Shinto Deity in the Guise of the Monk Hyeja
by Japan
Japan · Japanese (culture or style) · 1001–1125 · Magnolia wood with traces of pigment
Bowl with Mandarin Ducks in a Lotus Pond
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 1001–1100 · Ding ware; porcelain with underglaze carved decoration; metal rim
Dazu Rock Carvings
Tang to Song · 9th–13th c. · Sichuan
Buddhist, Daoist, and Confucian figures carved side by side — a rare moment of syncretism preserved in stone.
Byōdō-in
Late Heian · 1053 · Uji
The Phoenix Hall — bird-shaped pavilion reflecting in its lotus pond. Built as a noble's villa; converted to a temple when he took the tonsure.
Phoenix Hall of Byōdō-in
Heian · 1053 · Uji, Japan
Fujiwara no Yorimichi built the hall as an earthly image of Amida's Western Paradise, floating above a pond on narrow wings like a descending bird. Its central image of Amida is the only surviving work of the master sculptor Jōchō.
Early Spring
by Guo Xi
Northern Song · Guo Xi · 1072 · China
A mountain landscape in ink and subtle color. Guo Xi said a painter should look at nature "as if going on a journey" — this scroll is that walk.
Brush Washer
China, Henan Province, Baofeng, Qingliangsi, Northern Song dynasty (960-1127) · late 1000s–1127 · porcelaneous stoneware, Ru ware
Old Trees, Level Distance
by Guo Xi
Northern Song · Guo Xi · c. 1080
A low-banked landscape: withered trees in the foreground, a misty river, mountains lost in fog. Guo Xi's essay "Lofty Record of Forests and Streams" defined the values the scroll embodies.
Flying Apsaras (Hiten)
Heian Japan · 11th–12th c.
A carved wood Hiten — a heavenly being playing a flute, suspended on drapery curling like smoke. Once attached as decoration to the ceiling of a Pure Land Buddhist hall.
Tea Bowl
China, Fujian province, Southern Song dynasty (1127-1279) · 1100s–1200s · Stoneware with black “hare’s fur” glaze and metal-bound rim, Jian ware

Ewer: Cizhou ware
China, Northern Song dynasty (960-1127) · 1105 · buff stoneware with underglaze slip coating
Auspicious Cranes
by Emperor Huizong
Northern Song · Emperor Huizong · 1112
Twenty cranes circle the roof of the imperial palace — Huizong, an emperor who preferred painting to governing, records an auspicious omen in careful mineral pigment. He would lose the empire within fifteen years.
A Thousand Li of Rivers and Mountains
by Wang Ximeng
Wang Ximeng · 1113 · Palace Museum, Beijing
An 11.9-meter Song handscroll painted in mineral blue and green by a student of eighteen who died shortly after. Hardly exhibited even in China because of the fragility of its malachite and azurite pigments.
Tomb slab
China · 1115-1234

by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 1115–1234 · Cizhou ware; stoneware
Tale of Genji Scroll
Late Heian · early 12th c. · Japan
The oldest illustrated version of Murasaki Shikibu's Genji. The "blown-off roof" perspective looks down into private chambers; faces are reduced to lines, clothing is everything.
Wind in Pines among a Myriad Valleys
by Li Tang
Southern Song · Li Tang · 1124
Li Tang's huge hanging scroll of misty pines and cliffs — a turning point in Chinese landscape, where the brush began to behave like the wind it described.
Basin
China, Zhejiang province, Hangzhou, Southern Song dynasty (1127-1279) · 1127–1279 · porcelaneous stoneware, Guan ware
Brush Rest
Southeast China, Zhejiang province, Southern Song dynasty (1127–1279) · 1127–1279 · glazed stoneware, Guan ware
Bracket-Lobed Dish
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 1127–1279 · Jun ware; glazed stoneware
Cloudy Mountains (Mi Youren)
by Mi Youren
Southern Song · Mi Youren · 1130
A handscroll of mist-wrapped peaks, executed in the "Mi dots" technique his father invented — layered wet blobs of ink building volume through atmospheric haze alone.
Goryeo Celadon
Goryeo dynasty · 10th–14th c. · Korea
Stoneware with a jade-green glaze so admired that Song-dynasty Chinese called Korean celadon the "first under heaven."
Along the River During Qingming
by Zhang Zeduan
Northern Song · Zhang Zeduan · 12th c.
A five-meter handscroll showing hundreds of people in the Song capital on a spring festival day. Read right to left — it unfolds as you walk past.
A Diplomatic Mission to the Jin
by Yang Bangji
Southern Song · Yang Bangji · c. 1150s
Song envoys crossing the Yellow River on the way to the Jurchen Jin court — a handscroll commemorating a humiliating diplomatic trip required by a treaty imposed after the northern lands were lost.
Melon-shaped Celadon Prunus Vase (Maebyeong) with Peony and Lotus Design Painted in Underglaze Iron Brown
Korea · 1150-1200
Processional Mask of a Bodhisattva
Japan, Heian period (794–1185) · late 1100s · wood, lacquered and painted
Itsukushima Shrine
Shinto · 1168 · Miyajima, Japan
A vermilion Shinto complex on stilts in a tidal bay, with its O-torii gate standing in the water at high tide. The whole island has been considered sacred for so long that births and deaths are forbidden there.
Repository for the Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra and Eleven Volumes of the Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra
Japan, Heian period (794–1185) · late 1100s · lacquered wood with ink, color, gold, cut gold, and metalwork
Plum Vase (Maebyeong) with Clouds, Cranes, and Children Motifs
by Korea
Korea · Korean (culture or style) · 1175–1199 · Stoneware with red and white slip and celadon glaze
Ban Dainagon Ekotoba
Heian Japan · 12th c.
A handscroll of a political conspiracy that ended in exile — a minister's slander, fire at the palace gate, panicked commoners. A masterpiece of Japanese narrative picture-telling.
Chōjū-jinbutsu-giga
late Heian–Kamakura · 12th–13th century · Kōzan-ji
Four picture scrolls showing rabbits, monkeys, and frogs at human pastimes — sumo, archery, a Buddhist funeral. Often called Japan's first manga, the energetic ink line and biting satire of clergy are unparalleled in medieval East Asian art.
Legends of the Yūzū Nembutsu Sect
by Artist unknown
Japan · Japanese (culture or style) · 1185–1333 · Handscroll; ink
Drinking in the Moonlight
by Ma Yuan
China, Southern Song dynasty (1127-1279) · late 1100s–early 1200s · Album leaf; ink on silk
The Autumn Hunt
by Chen Juzhong
China, Southern Song dynasty (1127-1279) · c. 1201–4 · album leaf, ink and color on silk
On a Mountain Path in Spring
by Ma Yuan
Southern Song · c. 1200
A lone scholar strolling at the bottom-left of an otherwise empty hanging — pine branch overhead, bird in flight. Ma Yuan's signature "one-corner" composition: let absence carry the scene.
Portrait of Minamoto no Yoritomo
Heian-Kamakura · c. 1200 · Japan
The founder of Japan's first samurai shogunate in black courtly robes — a yamato-e formal portrait attributed to Fujiwara no Takanobu. Possibly painted while the subject was alive, possibly a century later of a century prior.
Jar
China, Jiangxi province, Jizhou kilns, Southern Song Dynasty (1127–1279) - Yuan Dynasty (1271–1368) · 1200s–1300s · Glazed stoneware with resist and slip-painted decoration, Jizhou ware

Sericulture (The Process of Making Silk)
by Liang Kai
China, Southern Song dynasty (1127-1279) · early 1200s · Handscroll; ink and color on silk

The Buddha Amida, from the triptych Approach of the Amida Trinity
by Japanese
Japan · Japanese (culture or style) · 1201–1300 · Hanging scroll; ink
Covered Cosmetic Box
by Korea
Korea · Korean (culture or style) · 1201–1300 · Stoneware with white slip and celadon glaze
Rectangular Pillow with Figural and Landscape Scenes, Double-Lotus Medallions, and (back) Tiger Seated by Trees
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 1201–1234 · Cizhou ware; stoneware
Streams and Mountains with a Clear Distant View
by Xia Gui
Southern Song · Xia Gui · early 13th c.
A long ink handscroll in the "one-corner" style — only a fraction of the silk is painted, leaving vast voids for the viewer to read as mist and distance. A founding image of Chinese minimalism.
Fudō Myōō (Kaikei)
by Kaikei
Kamakura Japan · Kaikei · early 13th c.
A lacquered cypress-wood Fudō Myōō with sword and rope, eyes glaring, one fang up and one down. By Kaikei, one of the paired sculptors who defined the Kamakura Buddhist style.
The Knickknack Peddler
by Li Song
China, Southern Song dynasty (1127-1279) · 1212 · Album leaf; ink and color on silk
Tripitaka Koreana
Goryeo · 1236–1251 · Haeinsa
81,258 woodblocks of the Buddhist canon carved to petition the gods to repel the Mongols. The blocks still print; stored in a 15th-century hall that manages its own humidity.
Dragon (Muqi)
by Muqi
Southern Song · Fachang Muqi · c. 1250
A dragon coiling out of inked cloud — one of a paired scroll with Muqi's Tiger. Muqi was a Zen monk painter, much admired in Japan, where these two scrolls became standards of Zen taste.
Tiger (Muqi)
by Muqi
Southern Song · Fachang Muqi · c. 1250
A crouching tiger done in splashed ink — paw half-raised. Paired with Muqi's Dragon; the two represent complementary powers in Chan/Zen iconography.
Zaō Gongen
Kamakura Japan · 13th c.
A wooden sculpture of Zaō Gongen — a Shinto-Buddhist syncretic deity of the Kinpusen mountain. Right leg stamping the earth, sword raised, hair standing on end.
Dragon; Tiger
by Fachang Muqi
China, Southern Song dynasty (1127-1279) · c. 1250–79 · Pair of hanging scrolls; ink on silk
Nyoirin Kannon
by Artist unknown
Japan · Japanese (culture or style) · 1250–1330 · Wood with lacquer and gilding
Bamboo in the Wind
by Puming
China, Yuan dynasty (1271-1368) · 1300s · Hanging scroll; ink on silk
Foliate Dish with Crane and Deer Amid Clouds

by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 1250–1299 · Longquan ware; celadon-glazed stoneware with underglaze carved and mold-impressed decoration
Foliate Dish with Bovine (Xiniu) Gazing at a Crescent Moon
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 1250–1299 · Longquan ware; stoneware with underglaze molded decoration
Vase with Dragon and Bonghwang (Mythical Birds) Motifs
by Korea
Korea · Korean (culture or style) · 1251–1349 · Stoneware with red and white slip and celadon glaze
Great Buddha of Kamakura
Kamakura period · 1252 · Japan
A bronze Amida Buddha originally housed in a great hall — but tsunamis washed the building away in 1498 and left the statue to sit in the open ever since.
Poem by Wang Wei in the Cursive Script Style
by Song Lizong
China, Southern Song dynasty (1127-1279) · 1256 · Album leaf; ink on silk
Portrait of a Gentleman
by Artist unknown
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 1260–1644 · Album leaf; ink and colors on silk
Sanjūsangen-dō
Kamakura period · 1266 · Kyoto
A 390-foot hall holding 1,001 gilded statues of the thousand-armed Kannon. Each face is different; pilgrims look for their own.
Amida Nyorai (Kōshun)
by Kōshun
Kamakura Japan · Kōshun · 1269
A gilded wood Amida Buddha, the mudra of welcoming, eyes inlaid with crystal. By Kōshun, a Kamakura sculptor who inherited Unkei's workshop but pursued quieter interiors.
Bamboo, Rocks and Lonely Orchids
by Zhao Mengfu
China, Yuan dynasty (1271-1368) · 1271–1368 · Handscroll; ink on paper

Swift Bull from the Ten Swift Bulls
Japan, Kamakura period (1185–1333) · late 1200s–early 1300s · section of a handscroll mounted as a hanging scroll; ink and slight color on paper
Seated Arhat with Two Attendants
China, Southern Song dynasty (1127-1279) · late 1200s · Hanging scroll; ink and color on silk
Ten Verses on Oxherding
Kamakura Japan · 1278
A Zen handscroll illustrating the parable of a man seeking an ox — mind, self, enlightenment. The text pairs Chinese calligraphy with a seated ox and a boy, finally merging.
Yang Pu Moving His Family
by Artist Unknown
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 1279–1368 · Handscroll; ink and light color on paper
Vajrapani Embroidered Mount with Garuda
by Choying Dorje, the Tenth Black Hat Karmapa
China and Tibet, Embroidery: China, Yuan dynasty (1279-1368); painting: Tibet, 17th century · painting 1600s, embroidery c. 1300 · Opaque watercolor, gold, and silver on silk
Samguk Yusa
Goryeo · 13th c. · Korea
"Memorabilia of the Three Kingdoms" — a monk's compilation of legends and history. The main surviving source for Korea's founding myths and for early Korean Buddhism.
Heiji Monogatari Emaki
Kamakura · late 13th century · MFA Boston and Tokyo National Museum
Handscrolls illustrating the 1159 Heiji Disturbance, a coup attempt that left much of Kyoto burning. The burning-palace scroll unrolls into perhaps the greatest battle scene in Japanese art, smoke curling above warriors climbing a wheeled carriage.
Summer Mountains (after Dong Yuan [active c. 937–75])
by Huang Gongwang
China, Yuan dynasty (1271-1368) · 1290–1354 · hanging scroll; ink and slight color on silk
Round Box with Bird and Peonies Design

by Zhao Mengfu
China, Yuan dynasty (1271-1368) · c. 1302–3 · Fan painting mounted as album leaf; ink and color on silk
Shigaraki Ware
Muromachi onward · 13th c. · Shiga
Earthy brown stoneware with feldspar eruptions on the surface. Prized for tea — the accidental "fire colors" and ash deposits are the whole point.

Korea, Goryeo dynasty (918–1392) · 1300s · wood with lacquer and gilding
Song of the Stone Drums
by Xianyu Shu
Yuan China · Xianyu Shu · 1301
A handscroll of calligraphy transcribing Han Yu's Tang poem — the Stone Drums, ancient inscribed boulders that schoolboys once played on. Xianyu Shu wrote it in one long, thickening brushstroke.
Foliate Cup and Stand
by China
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 1301–1368 · Longquan ware; stoneware with underglaze molded decoration

by Wang Meng
China, Yuan dynasty (1271-1368) · c. 1308–85 · Album leaf; ink and color on paper
Horses and Grooms (Zhao Mengfu)
by Zhao Mengfu
Yuan · Zhao Mengfu · early 14th c.
Three horses and their handlers, painted in the archaic Tang manner by the leading Yuan scholar-official. Zhao turned away from Song realism toward brush-and-ink revivalism — and set the course of literati painting.
Goryeo Celadon Bowl
Goryeo · early 14th c. · Korea
Pale blue-green celadon inlaid with white and black slip — cranes in flight, chrysanthemums, clouds, rain. The Goryeo inlay technique had no equivalent in China; it is Korea's signature contribution to East Asian ceramics.
Prince Shōtoku at Age Two
Kamakura Japan · early 14th c.
A small wooden figure of Shōtoku Taishi, Japan's semi-legendary Buddhist prince, shown as a two-year-old in the traditional pose of hands clasped in prayer. Devotion in the form of an infant.
Three Horses and Four Grooms
by Ren Renfa
China, Yuan dynasty (1271-1368) · c. 1320s · handscroll, ink and color on silk
Vajrapani and Garuda
by Choying Dorje, the Tenth Black Hat Karmapa
China and Tibet, Embroidery: China, Yuan dynasty (1279–1368); painting: Tibet · painting c. 1650; top and bottom borders c. 1350 · Painting: gum tempera, gold, and silver on silk; lateral borders: silk damask with needleloop embroidery; top and bottom panels: embroidered silk, lead, and silver paper; curtain: silk tabby; dowels: wood, rawhide, and leather
Quails and Sparrows in an Autumn Scene
by Wang Yuan
China, Yuan dynasty (1271-1368) · 1347 · hanging scroll, ink on paper
Dwelling in the Fuchun Mountains
by Huang Gongwang
Huang Gongwang · 1350 · split between Taipei and Hangzhou
A Yuan-dynasty masterpiece painted for a Daoist friend, its 6.9-meter scroll burned in two in 1650 by a distraught collector trying to take it with him to the afterlife. The two surviving halves were reunited for one exhibition in 2011 after 360 years apart.
Openwork Braizer or Incense Stick Holder

by Korea
Korea · Korean (culture or style) · 1392–1910 · Ink and colors on paper

Kettle with Crane Design
Japan, Muromachi period (1392–1573) · 1392–1573 · iron
Kettle with Crane Design (lid)
Japan, Muromachi period (1392–1573) · 1392–1573 · iron
Gyeongbokgung
Joseon dynasty · 1395 · Seoul
The principal palace of the Joseon kings. Burned by the Japanese in 1592, rebuilt in 1867, mostly destroyed again under colonial rule — now being re-reconstructed, wing by wing.
Jongmyo Shrine
Joseon · 1395 · Seoul
The Confucian royal ancestral shrine of the Joseon dynasty, a 101-meter wooden hall whose austere horizontal roofline is the longest of its kind in East Asia. Twice-yearly rites performed here preserve court music banned in revolutionary China and Vietnam.
Jogyesa
Korean Seon Buddhist · founded 1395 · Seoul
The head temple of the Jogye Order — the principal Korean Buddhist school. An old pagoda tree in the courtyard is 450 years old; lanterns hang from it by the thousands at Buddha's birthday.
Kinkaku-ji (Golden Pavilion)
Muromachi · 1397, rebuilt 1955 · Kyoto
A retirement villa turned Zen temple, its top two floors wrapped in gold leaf. Burned down by a young monk in 1950 — rebuilt in facsimile.
Gathering at Lanting (Orchid Pavilion)

Meiping Vase with Cloud Collars and Peony Sprays
China, Jiangxi province, Jingdezhen kilns, Ming dynasty (1368-1644), Yongle period (1403-1424) · 1403–24 · porcelain with incised decoration and white glaze
Forbidden City
Ming & Qing · 1406–1420 onward · Beijing
980 buildings on 180 acres. For five centuries, commoners who entered uninvited were executed. It is now the world's most-visited museum.
Temple of Heaven
Ming · 1420 · Beijing
A round blue-roofed temple on a square base — heaven over earth. Every winter solstice for five centuries, the emperor prayed here for the harvest.
Eighteen Songs of a Nomad Flute
Early Ming · 15th c. · China
A handscroll illustrating Lady Wenji's Han-era poems — kidnapped by nomads, held 12 years beyond the Great Wall, returned south to grieve the sons she left behind. A favorite Chinese theme of exile.
Listening to the Wind in the Pines
by Ikkyū Sōjun
Japan, Muromachi period (1392–1573) · 1400s · hanging scroll; ink on paper
Pillars of the Country
by Yao Shou

by Shen Zhou, 沈周
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 1441–1451 · Handscroll; ink on paper
Ryōan-ji Rock Garden
Muromachi · late 15th c. · Kyoto
Fifteen stones in raked gravel. From no single vantage can you see all fifteen at once — you're always one short of a complete view.
Ming Chicken Cup
Ming · Chenghua reign · c. 1465
A small porcelain cup painted in underglaze blue and overglaze enamels — a hen and a rooster with chicks. One of the most legendarily expensive ceramic designs; a Chenghua chicken cup sold for $36M in 2014.
Wine Cup with Children at Play
China, Jiangxi province, Jingdezhen, Ming dynasty (1368-1644), Chenghua mark and period (1465-1487) · 1465–87 · porcelain with underglaze blue and overglaze enamel decoration, docai ("joined colors") ware
Dish
China, Jiangxi province, Jingdezhen, Ming dynasty (1368-1644), Chenghua mark and period (1465-1487) · 1465–87 · porcelain with yellow glaze
Lofty Mount Lu
by Shen Zhou
Ming · Shen Zhou · 1467
An enormous hanging landscape painted as a 70th-birthday gift for Shen Zhou's teacher — the mountain reads as the teacher's character. A scholar's portrait in the form of geography.
Yellow Chrysanthemums and Red Osmanthus in the Style of Wang Yuan
by Shen Zhou
China, Ming dynasty (1368–1644) · 1468 · hanging scroll, ink on paper

Mandala
China · 1479
Ginkaku-ji (Silver Pavilion)
Muromachi · 1482 · Kyoto
Ashikaga Yoshimasa's retirement villa, intended but never actually clad in silver foil. Its ascetic gray-wood restraint and the adjacent sand garden — Moon-Viewing Platform and Sea of Silver Sand — crystallized the Higashiyama aesthetic of Japanese tea culture.
The Gathering at the Orchid Pavilion
by Attributed to Tang Yin 唐寅

by Wang Wen
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 1497–1576 · Handscroll; ink on paper
Ink Landscape
by Attributed to Kano Motonobu
Japan · Japanese (culture or style) · 1499–1599 · Hanging scroll

1500 – 1800

Great Wall of China
Ming (current form) · 14th–17th c.
Not one wall but many, rebuilt across dynasties. The Ming sections visitors see today are a fraction of a system that once stretched over 13,000 miles.
Buncheong Ware
Early Joseon · 15th–16th century · Korea
Stoneware coated in a pale slip and then incised, stamped, or brushed with freely drawn fish, birds, and peonies. Its looseness horrified the Chinese-influenced court but enchanted Japanese tea masters, who smuggled kilns full of it home in the 1590s.
Susuki Grass
Japan, Muromachi period (1392–1573) · c. 1525 · Pair of six-panel folding screens; ink, color, and gold on paper

Mount Hua
by Tang Yin
China, Ming dynasty (1368–1644) · 1506 · hanging scroll; ink and light color on paper
Flung-Ink Landscape (Shūgetsu)
by Shūgetsu Tōkan
Muromachi Japan · Shūgetsu Tōkan · c. 1510
A splashed-ink (haboku) landscape — mountains, hut, and traveler emerging from broad inkwashes applied almost at random. The technique's restraint was the whole point.

Retreat on an Autumn Day, in Cursive Script
by Zhu Yunming

Returning Home
Japan, Muromachi period (1392–1573) · mid-1500s · hanging scroll, ink and color on paper
Water Container (Mizusashi) with Riverscape
Japan, Momoyama period (1573-1615) to Edo period (1615-1858) · late 1500s–early 1600s · Stoneware with underglaze iron oxide slip decoration (Mino ware, Shino type)
Qiu Ying Paintings
by Qiu Ying
Ming · Qiu Ying · 1494–1552
One of the "Four Masters of the Ming Dynasty." Rose from lacquer-worker to court painter. His handscrolls of palace scenes and mountain recluses are among the most copied in Chinese art history.
Old Pine Tree
by Wen Zhengming

Phoenixes and Paulownia
by Tosa Mitsuyoshi
Japan, Momoyama period (1573–1615) · late 1500s · One of a pair of six-panel screens; ink, color, and gold on gilded paper
Peacocks and Bamboo
by Tosa Mitsuyoshi
Japan, Momoyama period (1573–1615) · late 1500s · One of a pair of six-panel screens; ink, color, and gold on gilded paper
Eighteen Views of Huzhou
by Song Xu
China, Ming dynasty (1368–1644), Wanli reign (1573–1620) · c. 1588 · Album of eighteen leaves; ink and color on silk
Snowscape with Figures
by Kim Si
Joseon · Kim Si · 1584 · Korea
Bare trees, two men trudging through snow, a horse behind them. Kim Si's winter scroll — one of the finest surviving early Joseon paintings, from a period when few Korean paintings on silk survived at all.
Cypress Trees
by Kanō Eitoku
Momoyama Japan · Kanō Eitoku · 1590
An eight-panel folding screen with a single enormous cypress sprawling across gold leaf. The Kano school's monumental court style at its peak.
Shino Tea Bowl (Bridge of the Gods)
Momoyama Japan · late 16th c.
A rough, irregular Shino-ware tea bowl, white feldspathic glaze dripping over iron-oxide drawings — "Bridge of the Gods." The ideal of imperfection (wabi) that Sen no Rikyū taught his student Hideyoshi to love.
Nishi Hongan-ji
Jōdo Shinshū · 1591 · Kyoto
Head temple of the Jōdo Shinshū Buddhist sect. The Momoyama-period audience hall Shiroshoin is decorated with lavish painted wooden ceilings and gold-leaf panels commissioned by Toyotomi Hideyoshi — among the most opulent surviving interiors of the age.
Arrival of the Southern Barbarians
Momoyama Japan · c. 1600
A six-panel screen showing Portuguese ships landing at a Japanese port — exotic visitors in bell-bottomed trousers and high hats, accompanied by African attendants, disgorging Jesuits and trade goods.
Arrival of the "Southern Barbarians"
Japan, Momoyama period (1573–1615) · c. 1600 · One of a pair of six-panel folding screens; ink, color, and gold on paper
Tile Platform for Tea in Summer
Japan, Momoyama period (1573–1615) · c. 1600 · glazed stoneware with painted slip decoration (Mino ware, Oribe type)
Dish with Design of Three Wild Geese in Flight: "Nezumi" Shino Ware

Blue and White Porcelain
Yuan → Qing · 14th–19th c. · Jingdezhen
Cobalt under a clear glaze, fired to translucence. China's most-exported art form — copied in Iran, Turkey, Netherlands, and eventually the world.
Winter and Summer Flowers
by Kaihō Yūshō
Momoyama Japan · Kaihō Yūshō · c. 1600
A pair of six-panel screens — winter branches on the left, summer vines on the right — painted with rapid black brushwork on gold leaf. Kaihō was a samurai turned painter.
Momoyama Tea Bowl
Momoyama Japan · c. 1600
A thick, deliberately irregular tea bowl — raku or seto-ware, drunk from in the wabi aesthetic Sen no Rikyū articulated for Hideyoshi: the warrior as humble before the tea.
Bodhisattva Guanyin of the South Sea
China, Fujian Province, Ming dynasty (1368-1644) - Qing dynasty (1644-1911) · 1600s · Porcelain with white glaze, Dehua ware

Illustration of the Tale of Genji, chapter 5 Waka-Murasaki
by Studio of Tosa Mitsuyoshi
Japan · 1601–1700 · Shikishi
Oribe-Type Ewer
by Japan
Japan · Japanese (culture or style) · 1601–1625 · Glazed stoneware
Shino-Ware Ewer
by Japan
Japan · Japanese (culture or style) · 1601–1700 · Glazed stoneware
Nijō Castle
Edo Japan · 1603 · Kyoto
Residence of the Tokugawa shoguns during visits to Kyoto. Its "nightingale" floors squeak deliberately underfoot — designed to warn of intruders. The shogunate fell within its walls in 1868.
Himeji Castle
Edo period · 1609 · Hyōgo, Japan
The finest surviving Japanese castle, nicknamed "White Heron" for its white plaster. Its winding approach was designed to confuse besiegers.
Mt. Fuji Tea Bowl
by Hon'ami Kōetsu
Edo Japan · Hon'ami Kōetsu · early 17th c.
A Raku-ware tea bowl with a white glazed rim called Fujisan — Mount Fuji. Kōetsu was a calligrapher, lacquer master, and sword-polisher as well. A bowl meant to be cradled.
Inro Case
by Artist
Japan · Japanese (culture or style) · 1615–1868 · Lacquer
Demon
Japan, Edo period (1615–1868) · 1615–1868 · wood
Nikkō Tōshō-gū
Early Edo · 1617 · Japan
The mausoleum of Tokugawa Ieyasu, built with the lavish polychrome carving the Tokugawa banned elsewhere. The three wise monkeys are here, above a stable.
Mt. Qingbian
by Dong Qichang
China, Ming dynasty (1368–1644) · 1617 · hanging scroll, ink on paper
Katsura Imperial Villa
Edo Japan · 1620 onward · Kyoto
A villa and garden considered one of the finest masterpieces of Japanese architecture. Shoin-zukuri precision: every plank, every sight-line from every veranda, deliberately aligned.
Kōetsu Calligraphy Anthology
by Hon'ami Kōetsu
Edo Japan · Hon'ami Kōetsu · early 17th c.
Calligraphy by Kōetsu over a printed background of Chinese lions and crab-finger grass — a collaboration with Sōtatsu at their Takagamine artist community. Ink and design as one gesture.
River and Mountains on a Clear Autumn Day
by Dong Qichang
China, Ming dynasty (1368–1644) · c. 1624–27 · Handscroll; ink on Korean paper
Maize and Cockscombs

China, Jiangxi province, Jingdezhen kilns, Ming dynasty (1368-1644), Chongzhen reign (1628-44) or Qing dynasty (1644-1911), Kangxi reign (1662-1722) · 1628–1722 · Porcelain with underglaze cobalt blue decoration
The Bird's Nest Patriarch (Sōtatsu)
by Tawaraya Sōtatsu
Edo Japan · Tawaraya Sōtatsu · early 17th c.
A Zen eccentric sits meditating in a tree, birds nesting in his hair and beard. A tarashikomi wash technique in ink — Sōtatsu's invention, which left dried puddled edges on the paper.
Ten Bamboo Studio Collection of Calligraphy and Painting
by Hu Zhengyan

Wind and Thunder Gods
by Tawaraya Sōtatsu
Edo Japan · Sōtatsu · c. 1640
A pair of gold-leaf folding screens — Fūjin bagging wind, Raijin drumming thunder. Tawaraya Sōtatsu's icon of the Rinpa style, copied by Kōrin, Sakai Hōitsu, and countless descendants.
The Mazarin Chest

Ceremonial Uniform
China, Qing dynasty (1644-1911) · 1644–1911 · satin weave silk with gold metal thread embroidery; supplementary weft; black velvet; metal (brass?); lined in blue silk
Potala Palace
Tibetan · 1645 onward · Lhasa
Thirteen stories of winter residence for the Dalai Lamas — a thousand rooms, ten thousand shrines, two hundred thousand statues. Built where a 7th-century fort once stood.
Landscape in the Style of Dong Yuan and Juran
by Gong Xian
China, Qing dynasty (1644-1911) · c. 1650 · hanging scroll, ink with white pigment on silk

Landscape in the Style of Huang Gongwang
by Gu Tianzhi
China, late Ming dynasty (1368-1644) - early Qing dynasty (1644-1911) · 1649 · handscroll, ink and color on paper
Joseon Ido Tea Bowl
Joseon · 17th c. · Korea
A crackled-glaze stoneware bowl, made as an everyday Korean rice bowl but valued above all others by Japanese tea masters. Ido bowls traveled east as war loot and stayed as treasure.

by Korea
Korea · Korean (culture or style) · 1650–1750 · Stoneware painted in underglaze iron brown

by Yueshan Daozong
China, Qing dynasty (1644–1911) · c. 1660–1709 · Hanging scroll; ink on paper
Shugakuin Imperial Villa
Edo period · 1659 · Kyoto
Emperor Go-Mizunoo retired here to design tiered gardens climbing the foothills of Mount Hiei. The upper villa's dammed pond reflects borrowed scenery of the surrounding mountains — the purest surviving example of Japanese shakkei landscape design.
Dish with Bird on Fruit Tree Branch

Japan, Edo period (1615–1868) · 1668 · pair of six-panel folding screens; ink and color on gilded paper
Kakiemon Porcelain
Edo Japan · 17th c. onward · Arita
Overglaze enamels on a milk-white ground. The Kakiemon workshop at Arita invented a palette — iron red, green, yellow, blue — that Meissen and Chantilly spent decades trying to copy.

by Fan Yi
China, Qing dynasty (1644-1911) · 1671 · Handscroll; ink and color on silk

by Tamura, Suiō
Japan · 1688–1716
Ink Landscape (Self-Portrait)
by Shitao
Qing · Shitao · late 17th c.
Shitao paints himself sitting on a rock, brush poised, surrounded by hills he is still deciding to render. A Ming prince turned Buddhist monk; his theoretical writings on painting are still studied.
Landscapes in Various Styles after Old Masters
by Mei Qing
China, Qing dynasty (1644-1911) · 1690 · album leaf: ink and color on paper
Squirrel and Grapes
by Shen Yongling 沈永令
China · Chinese (culture or style) · 1694 · Hanging scroll; ink and color on paper
Landscape after Guo Zhongshu
by Bada Shanren
Qing · Bada Shanren · c. 1695
A hanging scroll by Zhu Da, a displaced Ming prince who became an eccentric Chan monk. Bada's landscapes are spare, faintly unhinged; his "crying eyes" signature on almost every painting.
Square Dish with Design of Plovers over Waves
by Ogata Kōrin
Japan, Edo period (1615–1868) · c. 1700 · Glazed stoneware with iron oxide underglaze slip
Spring Mist over Jiangnan

Shôki the Demon Queller, from an album of paintings of Shôki, Edo period (1615–1868)
by Artist unknown
Japan · Japanese (culture or style) · 1699–1899 · Album; ink on paper
Moon Jar
Joseon white porcelain · 17th–18th century · Korea
Large round jars fired in two halves and joined at the equator, allowing subtle asymmetries to soften each moon-shaped silhouette. Modern Korean painters including Kim Whanki built entire careers around the jar's quiet poise.
Plum Tree (Kōrin)
by Ogata Kōrin
Edo Japan · Ogata Kōrin · c. 1700
A hanging scroll of a gnarled flowering plum — the signature Rinpa subject, picked up from Sōtatsu and passed down to Hōitsu. Kōrin abstracted the tree to its flowering gesture.
Vase in the Form of Archaic Hu

by Japan
Japan · Japanese (culture or style) · 1701–1900 · Glazed stoneware
Rough Waves (Kōrin)
by Ogata Kōrin
Edo Japan · Ogata Kōrin · c. 1705
A two-panel folding screen of waves rendered in flat curving dark and silver — decades before Hokusai, Kōrin had stylized the ocean to a pattern of interlocked spiral claws.

A Vignette of Life on the Frontier
by Jiao Bingzhen
China, Qing dynasty (1644-1911), Kangxi period (1662-1722) · c. 1717–22 · Folding fan; ink and color on paper
Flowers and Trees of the Four Seasons
by Watanabe Shikō
Edo Japan · Watanabe Shikō · early 18th c.
A six-panel folding screen of seasonal trees and flowers — cherry, maple, pine — painted in the refined Rinpa manner that came down from Sōtatsu and Kōrin into the mid Edo period.
Chrysanthemums by a Stream
by Ogata Kōrin
Edo Japan · Ogata Kōrin · c. 1720
A folding screen of chrysanthemums leaning over a meandering silver stream — Kōrin's signature tarashikomi wash and gold-leaf ground in one of his late masterworks.

by Li Shan
China, Qing dynasty (1644–1911), Qianlong reign (1736–95) · 1747 · Hanging scroll; ink on paper
Summer Palace
Qing dynasty · 1750 onward · Beijing
Three square kilometres of gardens and pavilions around Kunming Lake. Sacked by Anglo-French forces in 1860, looted again by Boxer-era troops in 1900.
Netsuke
Edo Japan · 17th–19th c.
Miniature ivory or wood toggles that held purses to kimono sashes. Frogs, rats, mythic beasts — detailed enough to demand a magnifying glass.
Red and White Plum Blossoms
by Ogata Kōrin
Edo Japan · Ogata Kōrin · early 18th c.
A pair of folding screens: two plum trees, red and white, lean toward a stylized silver river of whorls. Rinpa-school decorative painting at its most distilled.

Dream of the Red Chamber
by Cao Xueqin
Qing China · Cao Xueqin · 18th c.
A classic Chinese novel in 120 chapters, commonly illustrated from Qing to today — the Jia family's decline rendered in garden pavilions, dying flowers, a jade inscribed with fate.
Colorful Realm of Living Beings
by Itō Jakuchū
Edo Japan · Itō Jakuchū · c. 1760
Thirty silk hanging scrolls — phoenixes, roosters, peonies, carp — painted in pigments so refined that individual feathers are rendered in rows of ungleibert circles. Jakuchū gave the cycle as a meditation on impermanence to his local temple.

by Torii Kiyonaga
Japan, Edo period (1615–1868) · 1789 · Triptych of color woodblock prints
Prints of Kitagawa Utamaro
by Kitagawa Utamaro
Edo Japan · 1790s
Utamaro specialized in the bijin-ga — studies of beautiful women — often just head and shoulders, in close-up. Scandalously intimate for the time.
Moonlit Night (Shin Yun-bok)
by Shin Yun-bok
Joseon · 18th c. · Korea
Two lovers beneath a willow, a narrow moon above — Shin Yun-bok's genre scenes eavesdropped on Joseon Korea's private urban life, where women and men met outside strict Confucian bounds.
Mirror with Handle, Decorated with "Double Happiness" and Five Bats

Actor Prints of Sharaku
by Tōshūsai Sharaku
Edo Japan · Tōshūsai Sharaku · 1794–1795
Kabuki actors caught mid-gesture, their exaggerated faces rendered in crisp black outline against mica backgrounds. Sharaku worked for ten months and then vanished.

Japan · Japanese (culture or style) · 1795–1807 · Ink and light color on paper
Hwaseong Fortress
Joseon · 1796 · Suwon, Korea
Jeongjo built this 5.7-kilometer wall to honor his executed father; the young scholar Jeong Yak-yong designed an innovative pulley-based crane to place each granite block. A complete construction manual recording every worker's wages survives from 1801.

The Dream of the Fisherman's Wife
by Katsushika Hokusai
Edo Japan · Hokusai · 1814
A woman entwined with two octopuses — the larger embracing her, the smaller kissing her mouth. Hokusai's most notorious shunga print; one of the earliest explicit pieces to influence modern Western art.

The Great Wave off Kanagawa
by Katsushika Hokusai
Edo Japan · Hokusai · c. 1831
A woodblock print sold for the price of a bowl of noodles. Mount Fuji sits small and steady under the claw of the wave. Hokusai was in his seventies.
Fine Wind, Clear Morning (Red Fuji)
by Katsushika Hokusai
Edo Japan · Hokusai · c. 1830–1832
Hokusai's other famous Fuji — a single triangular form lit crimson by morning sun, clouds streaming off the summit. From the same series as the Great Wave.

Takiyasha the Witch and the Skeleton Spectre
by Utagawa Kuniyoshi

A sorceress summons a gigantic skeleton across the ruined palace of Princess Takiyasha. Kuniyoshi's triptych stretched across three sheets and into legend — Japanese woodblock at its most lurid.

One Hundred Aspects of the Moon
by Tsukioka Yoshitoshi
Meiji Japan · Yoshitoshi · 1885–1892
A hundred woodblock prints, each with the moon in it — Murasaki at Ishiyama, a warrior beside a ghost, a courtesan sitting up in bed. Yoshitoshi's last series, published as the West arrived and woodblock died.

Orchids and Rocks (Yi Ha-eung)
by Yi Ha-eung
Joseon · Yi Ha-eung · 1888 · Korea
A hanging scroll by the Heungseon Daewongun — regent father of the last Joseon king, painted after being forced from political power. Orchids are scholar-gentlemen; rocks are steadfastness.
